Phase 02 · Personas

User Personas

Three composite personas synthesised from our primary research — representing the primary users affected by digital harm. Each persona guides distinct design decisions and helps the team maintain human-centred focus.

Why Personas

Grounding design in real people

Personas prevent designers from solving for hypothetical, idealised users. Ours are grounded in direct interviews, documented cases, and demographic analysis of who is most affected by digital harm in India.

Persona 01 · Teen User
Young Social Media User
17 years old Urban student Instagram · Snapchat
Goals
Express identity and connect with peers online
Share life moments without fear of harassment
Pain Points
Identity-based harassment after innocent posts
Abusive messages from anonymous strangers
Shame prevents reporting to parents or school
Design Needs
Trauma-informed, private reporting tools
Peer support mechanisms built into platforms
Anonymous blocking without fear of retaliation
Persona 02 · Elderly User
Elderly Digital User
67 years old Retired WhatsApp · UPI
Goals
Stay connected with family digitally
Manage finances independently via UPI
Pain Points
Highly susceptible to authority impersonation scams
No family safety net for suspicious transactions
Complex reporting systems are inaccessible
Design Needs
Voice-first, dialect-aware transaction confirmation
Simplified UPI onboarding with clear vocabulary
Family guardian mode for high-risk transactions
Persona 03 · Professional
Urban Working Professional
28 years old Tech-aware Banking · Social media
Goals
Fast, trusted resolution when fraud occurs
Transparency on report status after filing
Pain Points
Sophisticated scams bypass moderately tech-savvy users
No acknowledgement or status after reporting
Reporting flows buried and time-consuming
Design Needs
One-tap fraud reporting from home/lock screen
Real-time freeze capability within 2 minutes
Live status tracker with ticket number
Design Impact

How personas shaped our solution

Each persona maps to specific design requirements in our selected concept. The elderly user drove the voice-first accessibility pillar. The teen user shaped trauma-informed reporting. The professional drove real-time status and lock-screen accessibility.

P1
Teen User → Trauma-Informed Design
Private reporting, anonymous tools, and peer support features came directly from this persona's unmet needs.
P2
Elderly User → Voice-First Accessibility
Voice-guided transaction confirmation in regional dialects is a direct response to this persona's vulnerability.
P3
Professional → Real-Time Response
The cross-bank freeze and live status tracker are driven by this persona's need for speed and transparency.
7 User Type Segments

Our research identified 7 distinct user segments in the HMW framework — these 3 personas represent the most at-risk primary groups.

Victims of digital harmPrimary
Elderly usersPrimary
Vulnerable / at-risk usersPrimary
Users targeted by scamsSecondary
Non-English / regional usersSecondary
Users with accessibility needsSecondary
Low-literacy usersSecondary
Designing Against Digital Harm
